relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: The guilt/reparation offering is most holy.
Its blood/life circles the altar.
Its fat portions are brought near.
The inward portions are lifted.
They rise as fire-offering to YHWH.
The priests eat it in holy place.
One instruction joins sin and guilt offerings.
The priest receives the skin of the burnt offering.
Prepared grain offerings provide for the priest who brings them near.
Other grain offerings are shared among Aaron’s sons.
The peace/well-being offering receives its instruction.
Thanksgiving brings unleavened bread with oil.
Leavened bread also joins the thanksgiving meal.
One from each offering is lifted as contribution.
Thanksgiving flesh is eaten the same day.
Vow and freewill offerings may extend to the next day.
The third-day remainder is burned.
A holy meal must not be stretched beyond its boundary.
Flesh touched by uncleanness is burned; clean ones may eat.
Eating the holy while unclean breaks covenant meal boundaries.
Touching uncleanness and eating the holy is treated seriously.
YHWH speaks again.
Fat from offering animals is not eaten.
Other fat may be used for work, but not eaten.
Eating reserved fat breaks the ritual boundary.
Blood/life is not eaten in any dwelling.
Blood/life belongs to holy reverence.
YHWH speaks again.
The one bringing peace/well-being brings the offering personally.
Hands carry fat and breast before YHWH.
Fat rises; breast becomes priestly portion.
The right thigh is given as contribution.
The priest who handles blood/life and fat receives the thigh.
Wave breast and contribution thigh become priestly due.
This is the anointed portion of Aaron and sons.
YHWH commands priestly provision through generations.
The main offering instructions are gathered into one Torah.
YHWH commanded these offerings at Sinai in the wilderness.

contemplative_note: Leviticus 7 completes the first offering manual: repair, thanksgiving, vow, freewill, priestly provision, holy meal boundaries, and reverence for life.
